title: The Knife Sharpener of Old Lyon
summary: A traveller describes meeting Monsieur Garnier, an old knife sharpener who still pushes his wooden cart through the narrow streets of Old Lyon and refuses to retire.
body: Every Tuesday morning, Monsieur Garnier walks slowly through the old streets of Lyon with his wooden cart. He has been sharpening knives for more than fifty years. People say he is the last knife sharpener in the neighbourhood. I met him last spring near a small bakery. He was wearing a blue apron and his hands were rough but steady. I asked him why he doesn't retire. He smiled and told me he has tried twice, but after a few weeks at home he always comes back. He said the streets are quieter than before and many of his old customers have moved away. Some of them used to bring him cakes when he finished their knives. Now most people throw old knives away and buy cheap new ones. He thinks that is a small kind of loss. His grandson visits sometimes and helps push the cart, but he does not want to learn the work. Monsieur Garnier is not angry about it. He says every job has its own time, and his time is almost finished. Still, he plans to keep walking these streets for a few more years, because the cart is lighter than his living room.

annotations.exampleEn: The farmer pulled a wooden cart full of apples.
She has been sharpening her skills for years.
The cook wore a blue apron in the kitchen.
His voice was rough but steady when he spoke.
My father plans to retire next year.
Our neighbours moved away last summer.
Don't throw old books away ‚ÄĒ give them to a library.

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect with 'for' to show duration
grammarNotes.bodyVi: D√Ļng th√¨ hiŠĽán tŠļ°i ho√†n th√†nh (have/has + V3) vŠĽõi 'for' ńĎŠĽÉ n√≥i mŠĽôt h√†nh ńĎŠĽông bŠļĮt ńĎŠļßu trong qu√° khŠĽ© v√† c√≤n tiŠļŅp tŠĽ•c ńĎŠļŅn nay. V√≠ dŠĽ• trong b√†i: 'He has been sharpening knives for more than fifty years.' nghń©a l√† √īng Šļ•y bŠļĮt ńĎŠļßu m√†i dao c√°ch ńĎ√Ęy h∆°n 50 nńÉm v√† vŠļęn c√≤n l√†m nghŠĽĀ n√†y.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use Present Perfect (have/has + past participle) with 'for' to describe an action that started in the past and continues now. In the text: 'He has been sharpening knives for more than fifty years' ‚ÄĒ he started long ago and still does it today.

grammarNotes.title: 'used to' for past habits
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'used to + V' diŠĽÖn tŠļ£ mŠĽôt th√≥i quen hoŠļ∑c t√¨nh trŠļ°ng trong qu√° khŠĽ© m√† b√Ęy giŠĽĚ kh√īng c√≤n nŠĽĮa. V√≠ dŠĽ• trong b√†i: 'Some of them used to bring him cakes' ‚ÄĒ tr∆įŠĽõc kia hŠĽć th∆įŠĽĚng mang b√°nh ńĎŠļŅn, giŠĽĚ th√¨ kh√īng.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'used to + verb' describes a past habit or state that is no longer true. From the text: 'Some of them used to bring him cakes' ‚ÄĒ they did this regularly in the past, but not anymore.

questions.prompt: When does Monsieur Garnier walk through the old streets of Lyon?
questions.explanation: The first sentence says 'Every Tuesday morning, Monsieur Garnier walks slowly through the old streets of Lyon.'
questions.hint: Listen for the day mentioned at the start.

questions.prompt: How long has he been sharpening knives?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'He has been sharpening knives for more than fifty years.'
questions.hint: Listen for a number of years in the second sentence.

questions.prompt: What did Monsieur Garnier do when the narrator first met him?
options: He was wearing a blue apron
He was sleeping on a bench
questions.explanation: The text says 'He was wearing a blue apron and his hands were rough but steady.'
questions.hint: Listen for a description of his clothes.

questions.prompt: How many times has he tried to retire?
questions.explanation: He says he has tried 'twice', but always comes back after a few weeks at home.

questions.prompt: Why does Monsieur Garnier always come back to work?
options: He cannot stay at home for long
He wants to teach his grandson
questions.explanation: He always comes back after only a few weeks at home, suggesting he cannot stay there for long.
questions.hint: Think about what happens after he stops working.

questions.prompt: How does he describe the streets now compared to before?
questions.explanation: He says 'the streets are quieter than before.'

questions.prompt: What did some old customers used to bring him?
questions.explanation: 'Some of them used to bring him cakes when he finished their knives.'

questions.prompt: What do most people do with old knives today, according to him?
options: Throw them away and buy cheap new ones
questions.explanation: 'Now most people throw old knives away and buy cheap new ones.'
questions.hint: Listen for what happens to old knives now.

questions.prompt: What is his grandson's attitude to the work?
options: He wants to take over the business
He helps push the cart but does not want to learn the work
He refuses to visit at all
He has already opened his own shop
questions.explanation: 'His grandson visits sometimes and helps push the cart, but he does not want to learn the work.'
questions.hint: Listen for what the grandson does AND doesn't do.

questions.prompt: What does 'a small kind of loss' suggest about Monsieur Garnier's view?
options: He thinks losing knives is a big disaster
He thinks throwing old knives away is a minor but real cultural loss
He thinks no one cares about anything
He thinks new knives are much better
questions.explanation: He calls it 'small' but still 'a kind of loss', meaning it matters in a quiet way.
questions.hint: Look at the adjective 'small' and the noun 'loss' together.

questions.prompt: Why does he plan to keep walking the streets for a few more years?
options: His doctor told him to walk more
He wants to make more money
The cart feels lighter than his living room
questions.explanation: The last sentence says he keeps going 'because the cart is lighter than his living room.'
questions.hint: Listen for the final reason at the end.

cloze.template: Every Tuesday morning, Monsieur Garnier walks slowly through the old streets of Lyon with his wooden cart. He has been {{b1}} knives for more than fifty years. People say he is the last knife sharpener in the neighbourhood. I met him last spring near a small bakery. He was wearing a blue {{b2}} and his hands were rough but steady. I asked him why he doesn't {{b3}}. He smiled and told me he has tried twice, but after a few weeks at home he always comes back. He said the streets are {{b4}} than before and many of his old customers have {{b5}} away. Some of them {{b6}} to bring him cakes when he finished their knives. Now most people {{b7}} old knives away and buy cheap new ones. He thinks that is a small kind of {{b8}}. His grandson visits sometimes and helps push the cart, but he does not want to {{b9}} the work. Monsieur Garnier is not angry about it. He says every job has its own time, and his time is almost finished. Still, he plans to keep walking these streets for a few more years, because the cart is {{b10}} than his living room.

blanks.explanation: 'sharpening' ‚ÄĒ the whole text is about his work of making knives sharp.
'apron' ‚ÄĒ a blue apron is the worker's clothing mentioned earlier in the lesson.
'retire' ‚ÄĒ the narrator asks why he doesn't stop working for good.
'quieter' ‚ÄĒ the comparative form of 'quiet' fits the idea of fewer people on the street.
'moved' ‚ÄĒ 'moved away' is a phrasal verb meaning to leave one's home for another place.
'used' ‚ÄĒ 'used to + verb' describes a past habit.
'throw' ‚ÄĒ 'throw away' means to put something in the rubbish.
'loss' ‚ÄĒ losing something valuable, even in a small way.
'learn' ‚ÄĒ the grandson does not want to learn the craft from his grandfather.
'lighter' ‚ÄĒ the comparative of 'light', meaning easier to carry; the cart feels less heavy than staying home.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Monsieur Garnier says the cart is 'lighter than his living room'. What do you think he means by this? Do you know any older person who feels happier when they are working than when they are resting? Why might that be?
